heroOverview: Myelitis is inflammation of the spinal cord that disrupts motor, sensory, and autonomic signals below the affected level. The unspecified form — ICD-10 G04.91 — is coded when the cause cannot be confirmed at initial presentation. Transverse myelitis, the most recognisable variant, affects approximately 1–8 people per million annually worldwide, but the broader category of unspecified myelitis is more common because many acute myelopathies remain aetiologically uncertain during early workup. The hallmark is rapid progression: weakness, sensory loss, and bladder dysfunction can advance from mild to severe within hours to days. About 1 in 3 patients recovers substantially, 1 in 3 is left with moderate disability, and 1 in 3 has severe, persistent neurological impairment. Early high-dose corticosteroid therapy and urgent MRI are the cornerstones of acute management, and the underlying cause — whether autoimmune, infectious, demyelinating, or paraneoplastic — shapes both treatment and long-term prognosis.

keyStats.prevalence: Transverse myelitis (the most studied subset) affects approximately 1–8 per million people annually; broader unspecified myelitis is more frequent
keyStats.demographics: Two incidence peaks: young adults aged 10–19 and adults aged 30–39; affects males and females roughly equally
keyStats.avgAge: Bimodal peaks in late adolescence and the third decade; can occur at any age
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 33,000 new cases of transverse myelitis annually in the United States; global data for unspecified myelitis are limited

definition: Myelitis, unspecified (ICD-10: G04.91) refers to inflammation of the spinal cord parenchyma from any cause when the specific aetiology has not been established at the time of coding. The term encompasses infectious myelitis, post-infectious or parainfectious myelitis, autoimmune myelitis (including the aquaporin-4 antibody spectrum disorder neuromyelitis optica and anti-MOG antibody disease), demyelinating myelitis within multiple sclerosis, paraneoplastic myelitis, and cryptogenic myelitis. Pathologically, inflammation disrupts myelin sheaths, axons, and grey matter cells across one or more spinal cord segments, producing a characteristic clinical triad of bilateral motor weakness, bilateral sensory disturbance with a spinal sensory level, and bladder or bowel dysfunction below the inflamed level. The condition sits within the neurological system and is primarily managed by neurologists, but the initial presentation often arrives in emergency medicine. Diagnosis hinges on MRI demonstrating a T2-hyperintense intramedullary lesion, together with evidence of inflammation in cerebrospinal fluid (elevated protein or white cells) or serological markers. Because multiple sclerosis and neuromyelitis optica-spectrum disorder (NMOSD) both cause myelitis, comprehensive antibody testing and longitudinal follow-up are essential to prevent a costly diagnostic delay.

typesClassification.description: Caused by direct viral invasion (enterovirus, herpes viruses, HIV, HTLV-1), bacterial infection, or parasites; often associated with concurrent systemic infection.

Immune-mediated spinal cord injury triggered by a prior infection (most commonly viral) or vaccination, typically beginning 1–4 weeks after the index illness; includes acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM) affecting both brain and cord.

Driven by aquaporin-4 antibodies (neuromyelitis optica-spectrum disorder) or anti-myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) antibodies; typically produces long, severe lesions spanning ≥3 vertebral segments.
Partial transverse myelitis affecting fewer than 3 cord segments, often asymmetric, with lesions at the cord periphery; may be the first presentation of multiple sclerosis.
Immune-mediated spinal cord injury associated with an occult malignancy, often driven by anti-CRMP5 or anti-amphiphysin antibodies; requires cancer screening.
No aetiology identified after comprehensive evaluation; applies to a significant proportion of patients and drives the G04.91 code.

primarySymptoms: Progressive bilateral leg weakness that can advance from mild difficulty walking to complete paraplegia within 4–21 days, the defining motor feature of myelitis.
Sensory loss or altered sensation below a defined spinal level — numbness, tingling, burning, or a band-like tightness around the trunk at the lesion level.
Bladder dysfunction: urinary retention requiring catheterisation is particularly common, affecting approximately 80% of patients with acute transverse myelitis.
Bowel dysfunction: constipation or incontinence depending on whether upper or lower motor neuron pathways are predominantly affected.
Back or neck pain at or near the level of the inflamed cord segment, which may precede weakness and sensory loss by hours to days.
Leg spasticity or involuntary muscle spasms as upper motor neuron injury evolves over days to weeks.
Loss of deep tendon reflexes in the acute phase (flaccid paralysis), transitioning to hyperreflexia and spasticity as cord oedema resolves.
Sexual dysfunction: loss of sensation or arousal, erectile dysfunction, or altered orgasm from autonomic and sacral cord involvement.
Lhermitte's sign: an electric shock sensation radiating down the spine or into the limbs on neck flexion, indicating cervical cord involvement.
Respiratory compromise when the cervical cord is inflamed at levels C3–C5, risking diaphragmatic paralysis requiring ventilatory support.
earlyWarningSigns: New back or neck pain developing alongside an acute viral illness or within 2–4 weeks of a respiratory infection.
A subtle band of tightness or tingling around the trunk, even without leg weakness, suggesting an evolving spinal sensory level.
Urgency or hesitancy to urinate appearing alongside the other symptoms.
A rapid increase in difficulty walking over a single day in a previously mobile person.
emergencySigns: Rapidly worsening paraplegia or quadriplegia with complete loss of leg movement and sensation — a neurological emergency requiring same-day MRI.
Respiratory difficulty, paradoxical breathing, or oxygen saturation below 95% in a patient with cervical myelitis.
Urinary retention with a grossly distended, painful bladder requiring immediate catheterisation.
Shock or high fever alongside spinal symptoms, suggesting pyogenic spinal cord abscess or infectious myelitis.
Acute onset at level above T6 with uncontrolled hypertension and pounding headache, indicating autonomic dysreflexia.

causes.cause: Autoimmune antibody-mediated attack (AQP4 and MOG antibodies)
causes.description: Aquaporin-4 antibodies (associated with NMOSD) and anti-MOG antibodies trigger complement-mediated destruction of spinal cord astrocytes and oligodendrocytes, causing extensive longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis lesions spanning ≥3 vertebral segments with severe clinical deficits.

In MS, focal inflammatory T-cell and B-cell infiltrates damage the myelin sheath around individual axons in discrete cord segments; lesions tend to be short (fewer than 2 vertebral segments), peripheral, and asymmetric, producing partial rather than complete cord syndromes.

causes.cause: Viral infection (enterovirus, herpes viruses, HIV, HTLV-1)
causes.description: Direct viral invasion of the spinal cord activates innate and adaptive immune responses; enteroviruses are the most common cause of acute flaccid myelitis in children, while HSV-2, VZV, and EBV can cause myelitis in adults, especially in immunocompromised patients.

Molecular mimicry between pathogen antigens and spinal cord myelin proteins triggers cross-reactive T-cell responses 1–4 weeks after an infection (most often a respiratory virus); this mechanism produces post-infectious acute disseminated encephalomyelitis as well as isolated myelitis.

In patients with lung, breast, or lymphomatous malignancies, tumour-expressed neuronal antigens drive an antibody-mediated attack on the spinal cord; paraneoplastic myelitis is suspected when myelitis develops in an adult without a clear autoimmune or infectious cause.

Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Treponema pallidum (neurosyphilis), Schistosoma species, and bacterial abscesses can cause myelitis through direct spinal cord invasion, vascular compromise, or surrounding abscess formation compressing the cord.

riskFactors.factor: Prior autoimmune disease (especially NMOSD or MOG-antibody disease)
riskFactors.description: Patients with aquaporin-4 or MOG antibodies have a high rate of recurrent myelitis attacks; ongoing immunosuppression is required to prevent relapse.

riskFactors.factor: Recent viral infection or live vaccination
riskFactors.description: Post-infectious and post-vaccination myelitis typically occur 1–4 weeks after the trigger; respiratory and gastrointestinal viruses are the most common antecedents in reported cases.

riskFactors.factor: Known multiple sclerosis or first demyelinating event
riskFactors.description: Patients with established MS have recurrent risk of cord relapse; a first myelitis episode associated with oligoclonal bands and brain MRI lesions predicts conversion to MS in up to 60–80% of cases.
Impaired cellular immunity raises vulnerability to viral myelitis from herpes viruses, CMV, and enteroviruses; antiretroviral therapy reduces HIV-associated spinal cord disease risk substantially.

HTLV-1 tropical spastic paraparesis is endemic in the Caribbean, Japan, and parts of Africa; schistosomiasis-associated myelitis is prevalent in sub-Saharan Africa; travel and immigration history are diagnostic pointers.
Neuromyelitis optica-spectrum disorder, the most common relapsing cause of myelitis, affects women in about 80–90% of AQP4-antibody-positive cases, with a female-to-male ratio of approximately 9:1.

riskFactors.factor: Personal or family history of autoimmune disease
riskFactors.description: Myelitis in the setting of lupus, Sjögren's syndrome, or other connective tissue diseases is well documented; these associations alter both diagnostic workup and treatment selection.

affectedDemographics: Young adults aged 10–19 (post-infectious myelitis, ADEM) and aged 30–39 (MS-related, NMOSD)
Women aged 30–60 for NMOSD, which has a strong female predominance
Immunocompromised individuals at any age (HIV-positive, transplant recipients, chemotherapy patients)
Children under 15 in whom enteroviral acute flaccid myelitis occurs after poliovirus-like outbreaks
Adults in HTLV-1 endemic regions (Caribbean, Japan, parts of Africa) for tropical spastic paraparesis
diagnosisOverview: Urgent MRI of the entire spine with gadolinium contrast is the single most important investigation and should be obtained on the day of presentation. The hallmark is a T2-hyperintense intramedullary signal change, most commonly in the thoracic cord. Gadolinium enhancement indicates active inflammation and helps date the lesion. Brain MRI is performed simultaneously to assess for lesions consistent with MS or ADEM. Lumbar puncture and cerebrospinal fluid analysis follow once cord compression has been excluded by imaging: CSF typically shows a pleocytosis (elevated white cells) and elevated protein in inflammatory myelitis. CSF oligoclonal bands support a demyelinating diagnosis. A comprehensive serology panel — aquaporin-4 antibodies, MOG-IgG, antinuclear antibodies, anti-Ro/La, anti-dsDNA, syphilis serology, and HIV — is drawn simultaneously. In patients with a febrile illness or travel history, CSF polymerase chain reaction for herpes viruses, enteroviruses, and arboviral pathogens is added. A paraneoplastic antibody panel and CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis are indicated in adults over 40 without another clear explanation. Nerve conduction studies and electromyography help distinguish myelitis from acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (Guillain-Barré), which can closely mimic early cord disease. The time window from symptom onset to MRI directly impacts treatment decisions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the diagnosis and anatomical level of spinal cord inflammation, quantifies lesion length, and excludes structural compression or vascular causes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in an MRI scanner for approximately 40–60 minutes; a gadolinium injection is given through a vein in the arm mid-scan. The test is painless but loud. Results are interpreted within hours in an emergency setting.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies concurrent brain lesions characteristic of MS, ADEM, or NMOSD, which have major therapeutic implications.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Usually performed in the same session as the spinal MRI; adds 20–30 minutes. The combination of brain and spine imaging provides the full picture required for aetiological classification.

diagnosticTests.test: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis with oligoclonal bands
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects spinal cord inflammation (pleocytosis, elevated protein), MS-associated intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis (oligoclonal bands), and infectious pathogens via PCR.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A lumbar puncture is performed with the patient lying on their side or seated leaning forward; local anaesthetic is injected before the needle, and the procedure takes 15–30 minutes. Headache after the procedure is common and usually resolves with lying flat and hydration.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the two most important autoimmune antibodies causing relapsing myelitis, determining long-term immunosuppression strategy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results using cell-based assays take 2–7 days. Early empirical treatment should not wait for these results in the acute setting.

diagnosticTests.test: Infectious workup (CSF PCR for herpes viruses and enteroviruses; blood cultures; serology)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies viral, bacterial, or parasitic causes where direct or immune-mediated infection is the driver of myelitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Multiple blood draws and CSF collection from the lumbar puncture; PCR results return in 1–3 days. Blood cultures may take longer to flag if bacteria grow slowly.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for underlying malignancy-associated myelitis in adults over 40 or when other causes are not identified.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw sent to a specialist neuroimmunology laboratory; turnaround is typically 5–10 business days. CT of chest, abdomen, and pelvis is added when the panel is positive.

treatmentOverview: Acute myelitis is a neurological emergency. The first decision is to distinguish it from compressive myelopathy — a cord-compressing disc herniation, tumour, or epidural abscess — because these require immediate surgical decompression rather than immunotherapy. Once MRI confirms inflammatory myelitis, high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g daily for 3–5 days) is the standard acute treatment, based on evidence in MS relapses and clinical consensus in other inflammatory myelitides. For patients who show limited improvement after 5–7 days of steroids, plasma exchange (plasmapheresis, 5–7 cycles over 10–14 days) is the most evidence-based escalation, rescuing approximately one-third of steroid-refractory patients. Intravenous immunoglobulin is sometimes used when plasma exchange is unavailable or contraindicated. Infectious myelitis requires specific antimicrobial therapy: aciclovir for herpes viruses, antitubercular regimens for TB myelitis, and antiretrovirals for HIV or HTLV-1. Long-term relapse prevention is tailored to the underlying diagnosis. NMOSD requires ongoing immunosuppression — rituximab, mycophenolate mofetil, or one of the FDA-approved monoclonal antibodies (eculizumab, inebilizumab, satralizumab) — to prevent attacks that are typically more devastating than MS relapses. MS-related myelitis is managed with established disease-modifying therapies. Rehabilitation is integral from the outset: physiotherapy, occupational therapy, neurogenic bladder management with clean intermittent catheterisation, and bowel programmes are started as soon as the patient is medically stable.

medicalTreatments.name: IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3–5 days
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose corticosteroid pulse therapy reduces spinal cord oedema and dampens the acute inflammatory cascade; it is the first-line acute treatment for autoimmune and demyelinating myelitis based on NICE and MS guideline recommendations.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Accelerates recovery from acute MS relapses, reducing time to regain ambulation by an average of approximately 2–4 weeks compared with no treatment; benefit in complete transverse myelitis is less certain but clinical consensus supports use.

medicalTreatments.name: Plasma exchange (plasmapheresis)
medicalTreatments.description: Extracorporeal removal of pathogenic antibodies and inflammatory mediators; 5–7 cycles are performed over 10–14 days for steroid-refractory myelitis, especially in AQP4-NMOSD or post-infectious cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves significant neurological improvement in approximately 30–40% of patients with severe steroid-refractory inflammatory myelitis in observational studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab (anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody)
medicalTreatments.description: B-cell depletion therapy used for long-term relapse prevention in NMOSD and MOG-antibody disease; administered as an intravenous infusion typically every 6 months, with dosing titrated to CD19 counts.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces the annualised relapse rate in AQP4-antibody-positive NMOSD by approximately 85–90% compared with untreated historical controls in prospective series.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous aciclovir (for herpes virus myelitis)
medicalTreatments.description: Antiviral therapy targeting herpes simplex virus 2, varicella-zoster virus, and cytomegalovirus — the commonest viral causes of acute myelitis — given for 14–21 days at high dose.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces viral replication and neurological progression when started within 72 hours of symptom onset; delay in treatment is associated with significantly worse motor outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Eculizumab, inebilizumab, or satralizumab (NMOSD-specific agents)
medicalTreatments.description: Three monoclonal antibodies with FDA approval for AQP4-antibody-positive NMOSD; each targets a different arm of the complement or B-cell pathway and is used for long-term attack prevention in patients unable to tolerate or inadequately controlled by rituximab.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Phase 3 trials each showed approximately 90–94% reduction in the risk of relapse versus placebo over 6–24 months in AQP4-positive patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical decompression (discectomy, laminectomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Emergency surgery when myelitis workup reveals a compressive lesion — herniated disc, epidural abscess, or tumour — as the true cause of the myelopathy. Decompression within 6–12 hours of deficit onset is associated with significantly better motor recovery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Motor recovery rates of 60–80% when decompression is performed within 12 hours of acute deficit onset for epidural abscess; outcomes decline sharply with delay beyond 24 hours.

alternativeTreatments.name: Neurorehabilitation and physiotherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured inpatient and outpatient rehabilitation programmes, including functional electrical stimulation, locomotor training, and task-specific exercises, form the primary pathway to functional recovery after the acute phase.

alternativeTreatments.name: Clean intermittent catheterisation (CIC)
alternativeTreatments.description: The evidence-based standard for managing neurogenic urinary retention; self-catheterisation 4–6 times daily maintains bladder health and prevents infection more effectively than an indwelling catheter.

alternativeTreatments.name: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS)
alternativeTreatments.description: Used adjunctively for neuropathic pain in myelitis recovery; evidence for benefit is moderate, but it is a non-pharmacological option for patients who wish to minimise analgesic medication.

whySeeSpecialist: A neurologist with experience in inflammatory neurological disease should be involved from the outset — ideally a neuroimmunologist or a neurologist with a specialist MS/demyelinating disease clinic. The diagnosis of myelitis is rarely complete at first presentation, and the long-term immunosuppression strategy for NMOSD or MS requires ongoing specialist input. Rehabilitation medicine specialists coordinate the multidisciplinary recovery programme, and urologists or continence nurses manage long-term neurogenic bladder dysfunction.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a neurologist at a centre that performs brain and spinal MRI within hours, performs lumbar puncture for CSF analysis, and has access to AQP4 and MOG antibody testing. For ongoing NMOSD management, look for a clinic with access to rituximab and the three approved NMOSD biologics. For rehabilitation, seek a spinal cord injury or neurorehabilitation service with physiotherapists trained in locomotor and functional electrical stimulation programmes.
preventionStrategies: In patients with confirmed AQP4-NMOSD or MOG-antibody disease, long-term immunosuppression is the only proven method of preventing future myelitis attacks.
In patients with MS, adherence to a disease-modifying therapy reduces the frequency of relapse-related myelitis by 30–70% depending on the agent.
HIV-positive individuals benefit from effective antiretroviral therapy, which reduces the risk of HIV-associated spinal cord disease substantially.
Travellers to HTLV-1 or schistosomiasis-endemic areas should be aware of exposure risks and seek testing if they develop neurological symptoms.
Prompt treatment of bacterial infections that can cause spinal cord abscesses — including tuberculosis and syphilis — prevents secondary myelitis.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain skin integrity: patients with sensory deficits below the lesion level are at high risk of pressure injuries and must change position regularly, use pressure-relief mattresses, and inspect the skin daily.
Follow a structured bladder programme with clean intermittent catheterisation at fixed intervals to prevent overflow, urinary tract infection, and kidney damage.
Exercise within the functional range as guided by a physiotherapist; aquatic therapy and cycling ergometers are commonly recommended for lower extremity strengthening.
Manage bowel regularity with adequate fibre, hydration, and a scheduled bowel programme to prevent constipation-related autonomic dysreflexia.
Maintain a healthy weight to reduce joint and skin complications in patients with limited mobility.
Join a peer support group for spinal cord injury or MS to reduce social isolation and improve psychological wellbeing during recovery.

recommended: High-fibre foods (vegetables, legumes, wholegrains) to prevent constipation and support bowel regularity
Adequate calcium and vitamin D to reduce corticosteroid-associated bone loss during pulse therapy
Anti-inflammatory foods (oily fish, olive oil, leafy vegetables) as a general supportive measure
Adequate protein intake to support muscle maintenance during periods of reduced activity
avoid: Excess sodium, which exacerbates corticosteroid-induced fluid retention and blood pressure elevation during pulse treatment
Excess refined sugar and processed foods, which contribute to weight gain and metabolic side effects of steroid therapy
Alcohol, which interacts with many immunosuppressive medications and worsens neuropathic pain

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is strongly encouraged in the recovery phase and is one of the most effective modifiers of long-term functional outcome. Physiotherapy-directed programmes typically begin bedside on day 1–2 of admission with passive range-of-motion exercises. As strength permits, standing frames, tilt tables, and functional electrical stimulation assist early ambulation. Target 30–60 minutes of combined aerobic and resistance activity daily in the subacute and chronic phases. Patients with incomplete lesions often regain ambulation with intensive locomotor training. Avoid breath-holding exercises or Valsalva manoeuvres in patients at risk of autonomic dysreflexia.
dailyManagement: Catheterise on schedule and monitor urine for signs of infection (cloudiness, strong smell, dysuria, new fever).
Take immunosuppressive medications at the same time each day; never stop them abruptly without medical supervision.
Inspect skin at bony prominences (sacrum, heels, hips) at every position change.
Track any new or worsening neurological symptoms and report them promptly — they may signal a relapse.
Attend scheduled neurology follow-up for MRI monitoring, especially in the first 2 years when relapse risk is highest.
prognosis: Recovery in myelitis is highly variable and closely linked to initial severity and the underlying cause. Approximately one-third of patients with transverse myelitis recover fully, one-third are left with moderate residual deficits, and one-third have severe persistent neurological impairment including wheelchair dependence and bladder dysfunction. Time to nadir — how quickly deficits worsen — predicts outcome: a faster nadir (less than 24 hours) tends to correlate with more severe injury. Partial cord syndromes recover far better than complete cord syndromes. NMOSD-related myelitis typically produces the most severe and potentially permanent deficits of all autoimmune causes, which is why aggressive relapse prevention is so important. MS-related myelitis generally has a more favourable recovery. Infectious myelitis outcomes depend on the pathogen and speed of treatment. Long-term, up to 60–80% of first episode patients with MS-like features go on to be diagnosed with definite MS or NMOSD within 10 years.
recoveryTimeline: The acute phase lasts 4–21 days, during which deficits can worsen before stabilising. Steroid or plasma exchange treatment aims to shorten this phase. The subacute recovery phase spans 3 months to 2 years: most functional improvement occurs in the first 3–6 months. Walking recovery, if it is to occur, is usually evident by 3 months. Bladder and bowel function tends to recover more slowly, sometimes over 12–18 months. Pain and fatigue can persist beyond the physical recovery. Neurological stability (no new lesions on MRI, no new symptoms) over 2 years is a reasonable milestone for assessing longer-term trajectory.
complications: Permanent paraplegia or quadriplegia, particularly after complete cord transection or severe NMOSD attacks, affecting approximately one-third of patients with severe presentations.
Neurogenic bladder leading to recurrent urinary tract infections, upper urinary tract damage, and renal impairment if inadequately managed.
Neuropathic pain (central sensitisation), which affects up to 40% of patients in the chronic phase and is often difficult to control.
Pressure injuries (decubitus ulcers) in patients with impaired sensation and limited mobility, potentially leading to deep tissue infection.
Autonomic dysreflexia in cervical or upper thoracic injuries, presenting as dangerous hypertensive episodes triggered by bladder or bowel stimulation.
Relapse: NMOSD attacks recur in approximately 50–75% of untreated patients, often with cumulative neurological disability from each attack.

supportResources.name: Transverse Myelitis Association
supportResources.description: U.S.-based patient advocacy organisation providing education, peer support, and a clinician directory for transverse myelitis and related conditions.

supportResources.name: NINDS — Transverse Myelitis Information Page
supportResources.description: Plain-language overview of transverse myelitis causes, symptoms, diagnosis, and research from the U.S. National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke.

supportResources.name: MS Society UK — Myelitis
supportResources.description: Information on how transverse myelitis relates to multiple sclerosis, including guidance on the next steps after a first episode.

sources.title: Transverse Myelitis Consortium Working Group. Proposed diagnostic criteria and nosology. Neurology, 2002
Wingerchuk DM et al. International consensus diagnostic criteria for neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders. Neurology, 2015
Weinshenker BG et al. A randomised trial of plasma exchange in acute central nervous system inflammatory demyelinating disease. Ann Neurol, 1999
Abboud H et al. MOG encephalitis: clinical and imaging features in adult patients. J Neurol, 2021
NICE MS guideline NG220 — Multiple sclerosis in adults: management
Jacob A et al. Treatment of neuromyelitis optica: review of 677 randomised controlled trials. Eur J Neurol, 2013

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ADEM involves both brain and spinal cord with multifocal lesions on MRI, whereas myelitis confined to the cord without brain involvement is coded separately; children and post-infectious triggers characterise ADEM more than isolated myelitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Multiple sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MS-related myelitis produces short, asymmetric cord lesions in a patient with other brain demyelinating lesions; isolated unspecified myelitis lacks confirmed MS diagnosis but may be its first manifestation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ALS causes progressive upper and lower motor neuron degeneration without sensory loss or bladder involvement, whereas myelitis characteristically includes a sensory level and bladder dysfunction.

confusedWithConditions.name: Spinal cord infarction
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vascular cord infarction causes sudden, painless deficit within minutes (not days), typically affects anterior cord motor pathways, and shows diffusion restriction on MRI rather than T2 inflammatory signal.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common cause of relapsing myelitis in high-income countries; a first myelitis episode warrants MS workup including brain MRI and CSF oligoclonal bands.

relatedConditions.name: Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis
relatedConditions.relevance: ADEM is closely related post-infectious demyelination that can involve both brain and cord; it is a key differential when myelitis follows a viral illness or vaccination.

relatedConditions.name: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Upper motor neuron signs overlap; distinguishing progressive upper and lower motor neuron disease from inflammatory myelitis requires EMG and MRI.

relatedConditions.name: Acute paralytic poliomyelitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Poliomyelitis causes anterior horn cell myelitis with flaccid paralysis; it is a historical and differential context, particularly for children with acute lower motor neuron weakness.

faqs.question: What is myelitis?
faqs.answer: Myelitis is inflammation of the spinal cord that disrupts motor, sensory, and bladder signals below the inflamed level. It causes weakness, numbness, and bladder problems that develop over hours to days.

faqs.question: What causes myelitis?
faqs.answer: Causes include autoimmune attacks (as in NMOSD or MS), viral infections, post-infectious immune responses, and rarely cancer-related inflammation. In many patients the exact cause is not found at first and tests are needed over several weeks.

faqs.question: Is myelitis the same as multiple sclerosis?
faqs.answer: No, but MS is one cause of myelitis. A first myelitis episode may be the start of MS, NMOSD, or another condition — or it may be a one-time event. Brain MRI and antibody tests help distinguish between them.

faqs.question: Can myelitis be cured?
faqs.answer: There is no cure, but most patients improve with high-dose steroid treatment in the acute phase, and up to one-third recover fully. Long-term immunosuppression prevents recurrence in autoimmune causes.

faqs.question: How serious is myelitis?
faqs.answer: It is a serious neurological condition. About one-third of patients are left with severe residual disability. Severity depends on how much of the cord is inflamed, the underlying cause, and how quickly treatment is given.

faqs.question: How is myelitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Urgent MRI of the spine with contrast is the key test. Lumbar puncture, aquaporin-4 and MOG antibody blood tests, and brain MRI are added to determine the cause and guide long-term treatment.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for myelitis?
faqs.answer: Acute treatment is intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3–5 days. Patients who do not improve may receive plasma exchange. Long-term immunosuppression (rituximab or other biologics) is used to prevent relapses in autoimmune myelitis.

faqs.question: Can myelitis cause permanent paralysis?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly in severe attacks or when caused by NMOSD. About one-third of patients with complete transverse myelitis do not regain independent walking. Early treatment improves the chance of recovery.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from myelitis take?
faqs.answer: Most improvement occurs in the first 3–6 months. Walking recovery, if it happens, is usually evident within 3 months. Bladder function and pain may take 12–18 months to reach their final state.

faqs.question: Can myelitis come back?
faqs.answer: Yes. In NMOSD, relapses occur in approximately 50–75% of untreated patients, often causing cumulative disability. MS-related myelitis can also recur. Ongoing immunosuppression is the most effective relapse prevention.

faqs.question: What is the difference between myelitis and meningitis?
faqs.answer: Myelitis is inflammation inside the spinal cord causing weakness and sensory loss below the level of the lesion. Meningitis is inflammation of the membranes surrounding the brain and spinal cord, causing headache, neck stiffness, and photophobia without spinal level signs.

faqs.question: Does myelitis affect the bladder?
faqs.answer: Yes, in approximately 80% of cases. Urinary retention is most common — the bladder fills but cannot empty, requiring catheterisation. Bladder dysfunction often persists after motor recovery and is managed with clean intermittent catheterisation.

faqs.question: What antibody tests are done for myelitis?
faqs.answer: Aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibody for NMOSD and MOG-IgG for MOG-antibody disease are the two most important. Positive results identify which immunosuppressive regimen is most appropriate and indicate high relapse risk.

faqs.question: Is myelitis painful?
faqs.answer: Yes. Back or neck pain at the level of the lesion often precedes weakness. Neuropathic pain — burning, shooting, or electric sensations below the level — affects about 40% of patients in the long term and can be one of the most disabling aspects.

faqs.question: What is transverse myelitis?
faqs.answer: Transverse myelitis is a form of myelitis affecting the full width of the spinal cord at one or more levels, producing bilateral weakness, sensory loss, and bladder dysfunction below the lesion. It is the most studied subtype of unspecified myelitis.

faqs.question: Can children get myelitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Post-infectious myelitis and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis are seen in children, often after respiratory viruses. Enteroviral acute flaccid myelitis is a distinct childhood illness causing sudden arm or leg weakness.

faqs.question: Is myelitis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most forms are not directly inherited, though a personal or family history of autoimmune disease increases susceptibility to autoimmune myelitis. Genetic factors influencing immune regulation likely play a background role in susceptibility.

faqs.question: Can myelitis cause sexual dysfunction?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sacral and thoracolumbar cord involvement impairs the autonomic and somatic nerve pathways for sexual function, causing reduced sensation, erectile dysfunction in men, and altered orgasm. These symptoms are common and should be discussed with the treating neurologist.

faqs.question: What happens at the first neurology appointment after myelitis?
faqs.answer: The neurologist will review MRI and CSF results, discuss antibody test results, confirm or revise the diagnosis, start or adjust immunosuppression, and refer to rehabilitation services. Repeat MRI at 3–6 months is usually planned to monitor for new lesions.

faqs.question: What is NMOSD and how does it relate to myelitis?
faqs.answer: Neuromyelitis optica-spectrum disorder (NMOSD) is an autoimmune condition characterised by recurrent attacks of severe myelitis and optic neuritis, driven by aquaporin-4 antibodies in most cases. It is one of the most important diagnoses to exclude in a new myelitis presentation because it requires lifelong immunosuppression.

metaDescription: Myelitis inflames the spinal cord, causing weakness, sensory loss and bladder dysfunction. Learn causes, MRI diagnosis, steroids and recovery. Doctor-reviewed.
